title-group.article-title: RNA export through the nuclear pore complex: pathways, mechanisms, and imaging strategies

abstract.title: ABSTRACT
abstract.p: Understanding how RNA molecules traverse the nuclear pore complex (NPC) is central to regulated gene expression because the NPC serves as the selective gateway for RNA export from the nucleus. Distinct RNA classes, including tRNAs, snRNAs, miRNAs, lncRNAs, piRNAs, rRNAs, mRNAs, and circRNAs, follow biogenesis-coupled pathways and engage specific transport receptors and accessory factors to cross this barrier. Recent advances in single-molecule and super-resolution microscopy now enable direct, NPC-resolved visualization of transport for selected RNA species, allowing quantitative measurements of export kinetics, 3D trajectories through the pore, and interaction dynamics with NPC substructures. Here, we review and compare what has been learned from NPC-resolved imaging across RNA classes studied to date, highlighting both shared organizing principles and cargo-specific behaviours. We then describe an experimental and analytical toolbox for NPC-resolved studies of mRNA and pre-ribosomal particle export, including RNA-labelling strategies, major single-molecule and super-resolution modalities, and quantitative metrics used to extract transport parameters. Finally, we discuss key technical and conceptual barriers that currently limit extension to other RNA classes, particularly small and/or low-abundance RNAs, and outline practical strategies to overcome these constraints. Together, this review provides a unified framework for the next generation of single-molecule dissection of RNA dynamics at the nuclear pore.

funding-group.funding-statement: This project was supported by grants from the US National Institutes of Health [GM122552 and CA279681 to W. Yang].

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: The emergence of a membrane-bound nucleus was a major evolutionary transition in eukaryotes, establishing distinct nuclear and cytoplasmic compartments. The nuclear envelope (NE) creates a physical barrier that spatially separates transcription from translation [1], enabling additional layers of gene-expression regulation. Because most macromolecules cannot freely cross this barrier, exchange between nucleus and cytoplasm relies on nuclear pore complexes (NPCs), large assemblies embedded in the NE that form the principal gateways for nucleocytoplasmic transport.

Among the most extensively transported macromolecules are RNAs. Cellular RNAs comprise both protein-coding messenger RNAs (mRNAs) and a broad spectrum of noncoding RNAs, including rRNAs, tRNAs, snRNAs, snoRNAs, piRNAs, miRNAs, and lncRNAs [2]. Importantly, mRNA typically represents only a small fraction of total cellular RNA, whereas rRNA and tRNA account for the majority. Many RNAs are ultimately destined for the cytoplasm, where they support translation and post-transcriptional regulation [3]. For example, mRNA carries genetic information from transcription to translation [4], rRNA provides the structural and catalytic core of the ribosome [5], tRNA delivers amino acids to the growing polypeptide chain [6], and miRNAs regulate mRNA stability and translation [7].

Decades of research have established that nucleocytoplasmic transport proceeds through multiple mechanisms [8], unified by transport factors that engage cargo and mediate translocation through the NPC [9,10]. However, the kinetics and pathways of RNA export – particularly how RNA-protein assemblies interact with transport factors and NPC components during transit – have been difficult to resolve using ensemble approaches. This picture has advanced substantially with the advent of single-molecule imaging [11–13], which enables direct visualization of individual RNA molecules (or ribonucleoprotein particles) at nanometre-scale spatial resolution and millisecond-scale temporal resolution. In parallel, the development of diverse RNA-labelling strategies has made it possible to probe dynamic interactions among RNAs, transport factors, and the NPC in living cells.

In this review, we bridge RNA biology and advanced imaging to synthesize what is known about the NPC-resolved export of eight RNA classes. While nucleocytoplasmic transport is a universal eukaryotic process, the mechanisms and imaging studies discussed herein primarily focus on opisthokonta (specifically, yeast and metazoans). We compare reported transport behaviours and kinetic signatures, summarize current labelling strategies and cutting-edge single-molecule/super-resolution modalities, and discuss the quantitative metrics used to extract transport parameters. We also highlight key technical and conceptual limitations that currently restrict extension to less-explored RNA classes. Collectively, this review aims to provide a practical and conceptual framework for future single-molecule dissection of RNA export through the nuclear pore.

sec.title: RNA nuclear transport ‘gateway’: NPC structure and functions
sec.p:
In eukaryotic cells, the vast majority of RNAs are transcribed in the nucleus, with notable exceptions including mitochondrial RNAs and certain viral RNAs, and many of these nuclear transcripts must be exported to the cytoplasm to function. Although export is the predominant direction for most RNA species, some pathways involve bidirectional trafficking or nuclear retention. For example, spliceosomal snRNAs can be exported and subsequently re-imported as snRNPs following cytoplasmic maturation steps [14,15], in Saccharomyces cerevisiae pre-tRNAs can undergo retrograde (cytoplasm-to-nucleus) import prior to the formation of specific modified bases [16], and selective tRNA retrograde transport has also been reported in human cells under certain integrated stress conditions (e.g. oxidative stress) [17]. In addition, many RNAs – including numerous lncRNAs and telomerase RNA – are primarily nuclear and can be retained within distinct nuclear compartments [18,19]. Nevertheless, for the majority of RNAs that act in the cytoplasm, export across the nuclear envelope is an essential step in gene expression [20,21] and occurs primarily through nuclear pore complexes (NPCs) (Figure 1) [22]. Figure 1.
Schematic representation of the nuclear pore complex (NPC) architecture and spatial organization. The left panel illustrates the cell nucleus encased by the double-membraned nuclear envelope, composed of the outer and inner membranes, which are perforated by numerous nuclear pores. The nucleoplasm (light purple) and cytoplasm (blue) are separated by this envelope, while the nucleolus resides within the nuclear interior. The right panel shows a cross-sectional view of a single nuclear pore complex spanning the nuclear envelope. The NPC is composed of multiple nucleoporins (nups) arranged into distinct subcomplexes, including the cytoplasmic filaments, outer and inner rings, linker nups, and the luminal ring that anchors transmembrane nups. Central FG-Nups line the central transport channel, forming a selective permeability barrier that mediates nucleocytoplasmic exchange. The nuclear basket extends into the nucleoplasm, coordinating cargo release and interactions with nuclear components.Text at the top reads Nuclear envelope. A large circular nucleus is drawn with a double boundary. A label at the upper left reads Outer nuclear membrane left parenthesis ONM right parenthesis, with a leader line pointing to the outer layer of the double boundary. A label at the lower left reads Inner nuclear membrane left parenthesis INM right parenthesis, with a leader line pointing to the inner layer of the double boundary. A label near the upper right reads Nuclear pore, with a leader line pointing to one of multiple small pore like structures distributed along the nuclear envelope. Inside the nucleus, the central oval structure is labeled Nucleolus. The surrounding interior region is labeled Nucleoplasm.\n\nThe image B showing an anatomical illustration of a nuclear pore complex spanning the nuclear envelope. The left side background is labeled Nucleoplasm and the right side background is labeled Cytoplasm. The nuclear pore complex is drawn as a cylindrical assembly crossing a vertical membrane region. On the left, a structure labeled Nuclear Basket extends into the nucleoplasm side. On the right, multiple labeled components are shown with leader lines pointing to specific parts of the complex: Inner ring Nups, Linker Nups, Central FG dash Nups, Luminal ring and Transmembrane Nups, Cytoplasmic filaments Nups and Outer ring Nups. No axes, units, or plotted values are present.An anatomical illustration of a eukaryotic cell nucleus showing the nuclear envelope and nuclear pore complex.

NPCs are massive protein assemblies embedded in the NE that span the inner and outer nuclear membranes. With molecular masses of ~ 66 MDa in yeast and ~125 MDa in vertebrates, they are among the largest macromolecular machines in the cell [23–25]. Structurally, each NPC is a modular assembly of ~ 30 different nucleoporins (Nups) [26,27] arranged with eightfold rotational symmetry around a transport axis perpendicular to the NE [23,28,29]. A symmetric scaffold – including inner and outer ring modules – defines pore architecture and frames a central transport conduit with an effective diameter on the order of tens of nanometres (often described as ~40–60 nm, depending on definition and state) [30–34]. The cytoplasmic and nuclear faces are further decorated by asymmetric Nups that form cytoplasmic filaments and the nuclear basket, respectively [35–37], providing interaction platforms for transport factors and RNA/RNP remodelling machinery.

Functionally, the NPC acts as a selective permeability barrier and the principal gateway for nucleocytoplasmic exchange. The central channel contains both structured scaffold elements and FG-repeat – containing nucleoporins (FG-Nups): the inner ring provides major anchoring sites for FG-Nups that establish the selective barrier [38,39], membrane-anchoring components secure the scaffold within the nuclear envelope, and outer ring assemblies – often built from Y-shaped complexes – provide architectural support and organizational platforms for peripheral Nups and associated transport machinery [40–43]. Leading models propose that FG-repeat domains form a highly dynamic barrier that restricts non-specific passage of large macromolecules while allowing limited passive diffusion of small solutes (often approximated as <~40–60 kDa, in a size/shape-dependent manner) and enabling efficient translocation of large cargoes when bound to nuclear transport receptors (NTRs) via multivalent, transient interactions [44,45]. For RNAs, export typically occurs as ribonucleoprotein (RNP) complexes, whose associated receptors and cofactors mediate productive interactions with FG-Nups to achieve selective transport through the pore.

Beyond trafficking [46], NPCs can influence chromatin organization [47–49] and gene expression [50–54], and they participate in genome maintenance by recruiting specific DNA lesions to the nuclear periphery to facilitate repair [55]. Disruption of NPC components or transport pathways has been implicated in diverse pathological processes, including infection, cancer, immune disorders, and neurodegeneration [56–62]. Accordingly, each RNA class relies on dedicated export factors and pathway-specific regulation to navigate the NPC barrier and reach its proper cellular destination [63–65].

sec.title: RNA classes and nucleocytoplasmic transport pathways
sec.p: Nuclear transport across the highly selective NPC barrier is mediated by multiple conserved nuclear transport receptors (NTRs) [66]. Receptors that drive nuclear import are commonly termed importins, whereas those that drive nuclear export are termed exportins [10]. For many protein cargos, importins recognize nuclear localization signals (NLSs), while exportins recognize nuclear export signals (NESs), assembling transport-competent cargo-receptor complexes. These receptors transiently bind FG-Nups, enabling efficient passage of transport complexes, including RNPs [67], through the NPC. Transport directionality arises from the RanGTP/RanGDP gradient across the NE. This gradient is maintained by RanGEF in the nucleus, which generates and sustains high nuclear RanGTP, and RanGAP in the cytoplasm, which stimulates RanGTP hydrolysis and thereby maintains predominantly cytosolic RanGDP [68,69].

This Ran-dependent scheme accurately describes many protein export events mediated by CRM1 (Exportin-1/XPO1), but RNA export comprises multiple, class-specific pathways. CRM1 contributes to the export of several noncoding RNAs (including subsets of snRNAs/snoRNAs) typically via RNA-binding adaptor proteins. In contrast, large RNAs are exported as mature RNPs that recruit dedicated export machineries: most rRNA-containing preribosomal particles exit the nucleus predominantly via CRM1-dependent pathways, whereas the bulk of mRNA export proceeds largely through the NXF1/NXT1 (TAP/p15) pathway [70–73].

sec.title: Nuclear export pathways for messenger RNAs
sec.p:
mRNA is a single-stranded RNA transcribed from DNA that serves as the template for protein synthesis during translation. Because the NE separates transcription from translation, it creates a physical gene-expression barrier that makes mRNA export through NPCs an essential regulatory step. Pre-mRNAs mature through 5′ capping, splicing, and 3′ end formation (typically cleavage and polyadenylation) [74]. Mature transcripts assemble with RNA-binding proteins (RBPs) into messenger ribonucleoprotein particles (mRNPs) and are subsequently exported through the NPC to the cytoplasm for translation (Figure 2) [75]. Figure 2.
Two representative pathways for nuclear mRNP assembly and maturation that generate export-competent messenger RNAs. (A) NXF1/NXT1-dependent export: co-transcriptional processing and packaging (5′ capping, splicing, 3′ end cleavage/polyadenylation) recruit adaptor complexes and RNA-binding proteins that load the NXF1–NXT1 export receptor onto the mRNP, producing an export-ready particle that engages FG-Nups for translocation through the NPC. (B) CRM1-dependent export: a subset of mRNAs is assembled into RNPs that recruit CRM1 (exportin-1) indirectly via NES-containing adaptor proteins, often coupled to specialized processing or regulatory contexts; the resulting complexes undergo RanGTP-dependent export through the NPC and are remodeled upon cytoplasmic release.The image A shows the NXF1/NXT1-dependent export pathway. Various components such as CFI68, ALY, THO, UAP56, CHTOP, DDX39, CIP29 and SR are involved in processing and packaging the messenger ribonucleoprotein. The NXF1 and NXT1 export receptors are loaded onto the messenger ribonucleoprotein, which then engages with TREX-2 for translocation through the nuclear pore complex. The image B shows the CRM1-dependent export pathway. Components like ARE, HUR, NXF3, IFN-a1, eIF4E, 4ESE and LRPPRC are involved, with CRM1 and RanGTP facilitating export. The messenger ribonucleoprotein is assembled and exported through the nuclear pore complex, undergoing RanGTP-dependent export and remodeling upon cytoplasmic release.Two diagrams showing pathways for nuclear mRNP assembly and export through the nuclear pore complex.

The primary and general pathway for mammalian mRNA export utilizes the conserved heterodimeric receptor NXF1-NXT1 (also known as TAP-p15; Mex67-Mtr2 in yeast) in a process that is broadly RanGTP-independent. Before engaging this receptor, nascent transcripts must be packaged by the TREX complex, which in human cells comprises the THO subcomplex [73], the RNA helicase UAP56 (also known as DDX39B; Sub2 in yeast), and the adaptor ALYREF (Yra1 in yeast).

mRNP packaging begins co-transcriptionally when UAP56 recruits ALYREF near the 5′ cap via the cap-binding complex (CBC) [74,76,77]. Recent structural breakthroughs demonstrate that ALYREF’s role extends along the entire transcript: it engages multiple exon-junction complexes (EJCs) via multivalent interactions, packaging the elongated mRNA into a highly compact, protective 3D globule [78,79]. Furthermore, ALYREF acts as a versatile molecular link, also coordinating the 3’ end processing of non-polyadenylated mRNAs prior to export [80]. Once the mRNP globule is fully packaged, ALYREF and THOC5 engage the NXF1-NXT1 heterodimer, exposing NXF1’s RNA-binding surfaces to load the receptor onto the transcript [81,82]. However, as this bulky mRNP translocates to the NPC, it must undergo dynamic structural remodelling to achieve export competence. Here, the DEAD-box ATPase UAP56/DDX39B functions as an ATP-gated molecular switch, orchestrating the direct handoff of the mRNP from the nucleoplasmic TREX complex to the NPC-anchored TREX-2 complex [83]. The timely release of UAP56/DDX39B during this critical transition is tightly regulated by both TREX-2 and the newly identified nucleoplasmic TREX-2.1 complex (comprising LENG8, PCID2, and DSS1) [84]. Upon arrival at the nuclear basket, additional factors, including TPR, coordinate with these remodelled assemblies to finalize the mRNP-NXF1-NXT1 conformation, thereby completing the canonical mRNA export pathway.

In addition to bulk export, a subset of mRNAs can be exported via a RanGTP-dependent route often described as a ‘specific’ pathway. CRM1 [85] does not bind RNA directly; instead, in the presence of RanGTP, it engages selected mRNPs through adaptor proteins [86], including NXF3, HuR, and eIF4E. These adaptors confer transcript selectivity while enabling translocation through the FG-repeat nucleoporins of the NPC.

Efficient and directional mRNA export is further reinforced by auxiliary factors that coordinate receptor loading, mRNP organization, and remodelling during translocation. The exon-junction complex (EJC) [87,88], deposited during splicing, promotes formation of export-competent mRNPs and supports recruitment of export machinery. CHTOP [89] modulates the ALYREF-to-NXF1 handoff to facilitate exposure of NXF1’s RNA-binding surfaces. At the cytoplasmic face, the DDX19-GLE1-IP6 module remodels exported mRNPs and promotes directionality by coupling ATP-dependent helicase activity to release and recycling of export factors. SR proteins [90] can function as adaptors or marks that enhance NXF1 loading, and CFIm68 (CFI-68) links 3′ end processing to export and can facilitate NXF1 recruitment. Together, these coordinated assembly, handover, and remodelling steps convert processed nuclear transcripts into transport-competent mRNPs and drive their unidirectional passage to the cytoplasm.

sec.title: Nuclear export pathways for ribosomal RNAs
sec.p:
Ribosomal RNA (rRNA) forms the catalytic and structural core of ribosomes, which decode mRNA to drive protein synthesis. After transcription and processing in the nucleolus, rRNAs assemble with ribosomal proteins and numerous biogenesis factors into large pre-ribonucleoprotein (pre-RNP) particles corresponding to the pre-60S and pre-40S ribosomal subunits. These preribosomal particles are subsequently exported through the NPC via both RanGTP-dependent and RanGTP-independent mechanisms (Figure 3). Figure 3.
The basic mechanisms of pre-ribosomal particles nuclear export. The schematic highlights that pre-60S and pre-40S use partly independent, cargo-specific export assemblies and can engage different combinations of receptors/adaptors, with several components best characterized in yeast. (A) Pre-60S (large) ribosomal subunit export. After nucleolar assembly and late nuclear maturation, the pre-60S particle recruits multiple export adaptors that create docking sites for export receptors—most prominently CRM1/Xpo1 in many systems—enabling NPC engagement and translocation. In yeast, additional export-linked factors/adaptors (e.g. Nmd3 as a key CRM1 adaptor, and other accessory factors) help stabilize receptor binding, promote directional passage through FG-Nups, and coordinate cytoplasmic release and remodeling. (B) Pre-40S (small) ribosomal subunit export. Pre-40S particles undergo late maturation in the nucleus and then recruit a distinct set of export factors and receptors. In yeast, export commonly involves CRM1/Xpo1 (via specific adaptors) and can also include additional pathways/factors (e.g. Mex67–Mtr2 and other accessory proteins) depending on maturation state and species. After export, cytoplasmic remodeling removes export factors and completes functional maturation.It includes components labeled NXT1, NXF1, NMD3, ARX1, CRM1 and Ran GTP. These components are depicted interacting with a nuclear pore complex, represented by a structure on the right. The image B showing a schematic of pre-40S ribosomal subunit export. It includes components labeled NXT1, NXF1, RPS15, CRM1, Rrp12, SLX9, LTV1, RIO2 and Ran GTP. These components are also shown interacting with a nuclear pore complex, similar to the first diagram. Both diagrams illustrate the process of ribosomal subunit export through the nuclear pore complex, highlighting the involvement of specific proteins and factors in the export mechanism.Two diagrams showing pre-ribosomal subunit export processes with components like NXT1, NXF1, CRM1 and Ran GTP.

The predominant pathway is RanGTP-dependent and relies on CRM1 (Exportin-1), which is recruited to preribosomal particles through adaptor proteins and NES-bearing assembly factors. For pre-60S export, NMD3 can serve as a key adaptor that links CRM1-RanGTP to the pre-60S particle [86]. For pre-40S export, CRM1 recruitment can be mediated through NES-containing maturation factors associated with the pre-40S subunit, including LTV1 and RIO2, and related factors [91–93]; RPS15 has also been implicated as a functional adaptor in this process [94]. Additional RanGTP-dependent receptors can contribute under specific contexts; for example, Exportin-5 has been reported to participate in pre-60S export [95]. In yeast, auxiliary factors further stabilize and regulate export-competent assemblies: SLX9 has been described as promoting formation of a stable transport complex [96], and the factor Rrp12 cooperates with CRM1 to support efficient pre-40S export and proper downstream maturation [97].

Ribosomal subunits have also been reported to utilize a Ran-independent route related to the bulk RNA export machinery. In this alternative pathway, the NXF-NXT1 heterodimer (Mex67-Mtr2 in yeast) engages FG-rich nucleoporins and has been reported to associate with both pre-60S and pre-40S particles [98], providing an additional mechanism to facilitate translocation of preribosomal RNPs through the NPC.

sec.title: Nuclear export pathways for other major RNAs
Transfer RNAs (tRnas)
sec.p:
tRNAs are short noncoding RNAs of ~70–90 nucleotides that decode mRNA codons and deliver the corresponding amino acids to the ribosome during translation. Although mature tRNAs function predominantly in the cytoplasm, tRNAs undergo bidirectional trafficking between the nucleus and cytoplasm, and this trafficking can be regulated by cellular stress. For example, oxidative stress triggers selective retrograde import of tRNAs in human cells [17], a process thought to contribute to tRNA quality control by retrieving damaged or improperly matured molecules for repair or turnover (Figure 4). Figure 4.
The typical nuclear transport mechanisms of different RNAs. (A) Transfer RNA (tRNA). (B) Long noncoding RNAs (lncRnas). (C) Small nuclear RNAs (snRnas). (D) MicroRNAs (miRnas). (E) Circular RNAs (CircRnas). (F-G) piwi-interacting RNAs (piRnas) in Drosophila melanogaster ovaries, left “f” refers to piRNA precursor transcript encoded by dual-strand piRNA clusters, right “g” refers to piRNA precursor transcript encoded by uni-strand piRNA clusters, such as flamenco (flam).The image A showing a ribonucleic acid hairpin like structure with multiple short horizontal bars across the stem and arms. Two overlapping circular labels at the lower left read Xpo dash one slash Xpo dash five comma Los one comma Ddp five dash Gle one. A separate overlapping circular label to the right reads Ran GTP. The image B showing a ribonucleic acid hairpin like structure with short horizontal bars near the top of the stem. A small filled circle is attached on the left side by a short line. Two overlapping circular labels near the left read CRM one and HUR. Text near the lower right reads AAA. The image C showing two ribonucleic acid hairpin like structures. The left hairpin has a small filled circle attached on the left side by a short line. Overlapping circular labels around the left hairpin read PHAX comma ALY comma uTREX comma DDX three nine comma and ATP. The right hairpin has overlapping circular labels reading CRM one and Ran GTP. The image D showing a ribonucleic acid hairpin like structure with short horizontal bars along the stem. Two overlapping circular labels near the lower left read Ran GTP and Xpo dash five slash Xpo dash one PHAX. The image E showing a single curved ribonucleic acid line forming a loop. Three overlapping circular labels near the lower left and right read IGF two BP one comma Xpo dash two comma and Ran GTP. The image F showing a curved ribonucleic acid line labeled Cuff near the left. Two overlapping circular labels near the left read THO and UAP five six. Additional overlapping circular labels toward the right read NXF three comma NXT one comma CRM one comma and Ran GTP. The image G showing a curved ribonucleic acid line with a small filled oval near the left end. Overlapping circular labels along the line read EJC comma NXT one comma and NXF one. Text near the right end reads AAA. The image H showing a schematic diagram of a nuclear pore complex spanning a horizontal double band representing a membrane. A large downward arrow points from the top side of the pore toward the central channel. The pore includes a top ring, multiple vertical elements and multiple oval and rounded elements arranged symmetrically around the channel. Several curved filament like structures extend from the lower side of the pore. No axis labels or plotted graphs are present.Composite schematic illustration of ribonucleic acid export complexes labeled A through H, including a nuclear pore complex diagram.

tRNA nuclear export is primarily RanGTP-dependent. In metazoans, the main export receptor for mature tRNA is Exportin-t (XPOT) [99], whereas Exportin-5 can export particular tRNA species or precursor/atypical forms by recognizing their structural features [100,101]. These receptors form RanGTP-stabilized export complexes (for example, RanGTP-XPOT-tRNA) that translocate through the NPC and dissociate in the cytoplasm upon RanGTP hydrolysis [102]. While Exportin-t and Exportin-5 represent the major human pathways, many other transport factors have been reported as auxiliary pathways for special export. This suggests both Ran-dependent and Ran-independent auxiliary pathways for specialized transport and quality control. In the Auxiliary or Yeast pathway, yeast receptor Los1, which is a homolog of Exportin-t, participates in both the primary export and the re-export of tRNAs, whereas Msn5 plays a specialized role specifically in the re-export process [103]. Moreover, the NXF1-NXT1 heterodimer has also been found to associate with tRNAs and mediate their translocation through nuclear pores [104,105]. Dbp5 directly binds tRNAs to facilitate their export. A process spatially regulated by Gle1 through the activation of Dbp5 ATPase at the NPC, CRM1 (Exportin-1) has also been identified as a bona fide nuclear exporter of tRNAs [106].

sec.title: Long noncoding RNAs (lncRnas)
sec.p: lncRNAs comprise a highly heterogeneous class of non-protein-coding transcripts longer than 200 nucleotides, with over 8,000 lncRNA species identified in humans. Structurally, many lncRNAs resemble mRNA, carrying a 5’ N7-methylguanosine cap and approximately half being 3’ polyadenylated [107]. However, lncRNAs lack protein-coding capacity and are more concentrated in the nucleus. They are critical regulators of gene expression, which involve histone modifications, transcriptional control, and molecular sequestration (sponging) of miRNAs and proteins, though the functions of most remain largely unknown [108].

The mechanism for lncRNAs export is less well characterized. Current evidence demonstrates that lncRNA export relies on adaptor-mediated engagement of the two major canonical export systems seen in other RNA types. For instance, a well-studied lncRNA, RMRP (RNA component of mitochondrial RNA processing endoribonuclease), is exported via CRM1 (Exportin-1), a process mediated by the adaptor protein human antigen R (HuR/ELAVL1). HuR recruits CRM1 by binding to the RMRP transcript at both its 3′ and 5′ ends [109,110]. Many other lncRNAs utilize the RanGTP-Independent mRNA export pathway. In oesophageal squamous cell carcinoma (ESCC), the lncRNA VESTAR is exported by the primary mRNA export factor NXF1 (TAP), often with the assistance of splicing regulators such as SRSF3, and this export activity has been linked to lymph-node metastasis [111,112]. Similarly, the lncRNA NKILA is exported via the NXF1/TREX pathway, dependent on defined cis-elements (CAR-N) within the NKILA and the recruitment of RNA-binding proteins like SRSF1 and SRSF7 [113]. Current examples underscore that lncRNA export is not uniform but relies on recruiting the established transport machinery via specific, lncRNA-embedded cis-elements and protein adaptors, which highlight a significant area of research with potential disease relevance.

sec.title: MicroRNAs (miRnas)
sec.p: miRNAs are a highly investigated class of short, non-coding RNAs, which are approximately 20–24 nucleotides in length. Functionally, miRNAs mediate post-transcriptional gene silencing via loading into the RNA-induced silencing complex (RISC). Nuclear export is the central step in miRNA maturation. Before this step, the process begins with the transcription of pre-miRNA, which is processed in the nucleus by the Microprocessor complex (Drosha/Pasha) to yield pre-miRNA containing a hairpin structure with a 2-nucleotide 3’ overhang. The pre-miRNA nuclear export is mediated by Exportin-5 (XPO5), which forms a complex with RanGTP. This complex directly recognizes the hairpin structure of the pre-miRNA and translocate it through the NPC [114]. Once it is released into the cytoplasm following RanGTP hydrolysis, the pre-miRNA then undergoes the final processing step via Dicer. This final step produces a mature, single-stranded miRNA [115]. Meanwhile, there are also non-canonical miRNA alternative export pathways. A subset of pre-miRNAs, such as those retaining a 5’m7G-cap miRNA, can be exported via the CRM1 (Exportin-1) pathway. Similar to other small RNAs, some miRNAs also use auxiliary factors to ensure quality control or manage export in specific cellular conditions.

sec.title: Small nuclear RNAs (snRnas)
sec.p: snRNAs are a group of non-coding RNAs that process the precursor mRNA (pre-mRNA), associating with a set of specific proteins to assemble into small nuclear ribonucleoprotein particles (snRNPs or ‘snurps’). These snRNPs then, together with additional protein cofactors and pre-mRNA substrates, form the catalytic core of the spliceosome. The spliceosome orchestrates the precise removal of intronic, non-coding segments from precursor mRNA and ligates the remaining exon sequences, generating a mature mRNA molecule competent for translation. snRNA export requires a complex that is generated via the synergistic binding of phosphorylated PHAX [116], the nuclear cap-binding complex (CBC), and CRM1-RanGTP [117]. Interestingly, CRM1 does not associate directly with any RNA cargo. Rather, it recognizes the leucine-rich nuclear export signals and combines with the CBC and phosphorylated PHAX [118]. Further, the transcription-export (TREX) complex also contributes to spliceosomal uridine-rich snRNA (UsnRNA) export during this process. At this stage, DDX39 facilitates the loading of PHAX onto RNA through the bridging function of ALYREF, before combining with CRM1–RanGTP [119].

sec.title: Circular RNAs (CircRnas)
sec.p: CircRNAs are characterized by a covalently closed loop that lacks free 5′ and 3′ ends [120,121], which have been reported to be vital regulators of gene expression with additional roles in cancer and immune disorders [122,123]. circRNAs are generated in the nucleus by back splicing precursor mRNAs and are predominantly detected in the cytoplasm. Thus, their nuclear exports represent a critical regulatory step. The typical nuclear export of circRNAs is reported to utilize a length-dependent export mechanism. In Drosophila melanogaster, this mechanism is HEL25E, while its human homologs, UAP56 and URH49, are required for nuclear export. The helicase UAP56 (DDX39B) promotes the nuclear export of long circRNAs, whereas URH49 (DDX39A) is required for the export of short circRNAs [124]. Further, it is reported that IGF2BP1 binds directly to circRNAs and recruits Ran-GTP together with exportin-2, thereby facilitating circRNAs export from the nucleus [125].

In addition to length-dependent sorting and general adapter pathways, recent findings highlight a new sequence-specific and context-dependent regulatory mechanism for circRNA export. For instance, Cao et al. recently demonstrated that adenosine (A)-rich circRNAs are actively retained in the nucleus of human pluripotent stem cells via the poly(A)-binding protein PABPC1, which interacts with the nuclear basket protein TPR [126]. However, during neuronal differentiation, a reduction in nuclear PABPC1 levels releases this restriction, enabling the nucleocytoplasmic export of these (A)-rich circRNAs, such as circRTN4(2,3), to promote neurite outgrowth and other specific cellular functions [126].

sec.title: Piwi-interacting RNAs (piRnas)
sec.p: piRNAs are associated with PIWI-family Argonaute and are essential for safeguarding genome integrity in the germline [127,128]. The piRNA pathway constitutes a conserved, small-RNA-based defence that suppresses transposable elements in animal germ cells. piRNAs generally originate from precursors encoded by genomic piRNA clusters, such as the flamenco (flam) piRNA cluster in the Drosophila X chromosome, as a long single-stranded transcript. Interestingly, the 42AB cluster in germline cells is transcribed as a double-stranded transcript rather than a single-stranded transcript. For dual-strand piRNA clusters in germ cell lines, the germline-specific adaptor protein N×F3 and the nuclear export receptor CRM1 are both utilized. Here, N×F3 recruits UAP56 and CG13741/Bootlegger to initiate transport. Following recruitment, the RNA cargo is loaded onto the complex. After formation, this complex utilizes the CRM1 (Exportin-1) route to exit the nucleus [129]. For single strand piRNA clusters in ovarian somatic cells, nuclear export of piRNA precursors requires an exportin module containing N×F1 and N×T1 that acts together with components of the exon junction complex (EJC) [130].

sec.title: Established pathways of retrograde RNA transport to the nucleus
sec.p: Retrograde RNA transport, also referred to as RNA import from the cytoplasm to the nucleus [131], is a selective and energy-dependent process which primarily involves noncoding RNAs. This process plays important roles in RNA quality control, maturation, and stress response. Mechanistically, RNA import typically occurs in the context of specific ribonucleoprotein (RNP) complexes: proteins associated with the RNA can provide nuclear localization signals (NLSs) and recruit dedicated import receptors, enabling translocation through the nuclear pore complex (NPC) under the control of the Ran GTPase gradient. Reported cargoes include tRNAs, 5S rRNA, lncRNAs, subsets of small nuclear RNAs, as well as viral RNAs and other exogenous transcripts [132–134].

Within this set of reported cargoes, small nuclear RNAs (snRNAs) provide a canonical example of export-coupled maturation, because their nuclear re-import requires two key signals: Sm proteins and the 2,2,7‑trimethylguanosine (m3G cap). Newly transcribed U snRNAs are first exported into cytoplasm, where they assemble with Sm proteins and undergo hypermethylation to a m3G cap in the cytoplasm. These maturation steps generate import-competent snRNPs that are subsequently transported back into the nucleus for spliceosome function. The m3G cap is specifically recognized by the adaptor protein Snurportin1, whose N‑terminal importin‑β‑binding (IBB) domain recruits Importin‑β to drive active translocation through the NPC via the interaction with FG‑nucleoporin [135–137]. Thus, the chemical modification of the RNA cap in this case is the trigger for nuclear import events. Notably, the m3G‑cap recognition by Snurportin1 is structurally and biochemically distinct from pathways that engage m7G-capped mRNAs, thereby conferring selectivity for snRNP import rather than indiscriminate re-import of bulk mRNA.

Other types of RNA also show evidence of re-entry to nucleus from cytoplasm. Retrograde import of mature tRNAs is a conserved pathway from yeast to vertebrates in normal conditions. It is first categorized in Saccharomyces cerevisiae and later extended to mammalian systems. This process contributes to stress response and RNA quality control. In yeast, retrograde import has been described for multiple tRNA populations, including aberrant tRNAs, fully mature tRNAs, and stress-regulated tRNAs. Mechanistically, tRNA reimport is generally mediated by importin-β family pathways and the karyopherin Mtr10/Kap111. Under amino acid deprivation stress conditions, the import process additionally requires Hsp70 chaperone Ssa2p [131,138]. 5S rRNA associates with ribosomal protein L5 to form an L5-5S RNP complex, in which L5 functions as a chaperone to promote recognition by the nuclear import machinery, including importin-β. In Xenopus oocytes, where 5S rRNA is produced at high levels early in oogenesis [132,139]. Emerging examples also suggest that certain lncRNAs can accumulate in the nucleus after cytoplasmic transit, especially non-circular mitochondrial-derived lncRNAs (e.g. MDL1AS, lncND5 and lncCytb) have been reported to associate with PNPT1 (PNPase) within mitochondrial intermembrane spaces, which supports their export from mitochondria. After reaching cytoplasm, these lncRNAs then further bind to HuR via specific regions, and HuR has been proposed to promote their nuclear accumulation by serving as a carrier that facilitates nuclear import [134].

Collectively, these examples highlight that nuclear transport often depends on state-dependent RNP remodelling and specific adaptor engagement, which requires the advanced imaging toolbox to resolve RNA motion, docking steps, and molecular state changes at the single-particle level in living cells.

sec.title: Imaging toolbox for NPC-resolved RNA transit
sec.p:
RNA nuclear export entails capturing the nanoscale spatiotemporal dynamics of RNAs through individual NPCs. A comprehensive understanding of this process, therefore, requires real-time visualization of RNA dynamics with high spatial precision and high temporal resolution. However, most single-molecule tracking and super-resolution methods are limited by the photon budget: they require a favourable signal-to-noise ratio and enough photons per trajectory to localize a particle repeatedly with nanometre precision. Meanwhile, imaging modalities must balance spatial resolution, sampling speed, and limitations such as phototoxicity/photobleaching under live-cell conditions. Thus, integrating appropriate imaging modalities and efficient labelling strategies remains a central challenge in achieving NPC-resolved measurement of RNA transit. Here, we introduce detailed updates in RNA labelling strategies and imaging modalities associated with its nucleocytoplasmic transport (as shown in Table 1), as well as current quantitative metrics for this RNA transit process, contributing to a consolidated imaging toolbox.Table 1.
Overview of single-molecule and super-resolution techniques applied to RNA nuclear exports.
Labeling StrategiesRNA TypeImaging MethodMajor conclusionsReference
MS2-MCP system fused to mCherrymRNASPEED MicroscopyAltering O-GlcNAc levels changes both how efficiently mRNA–protein complexes (mRNPs) and their transport receptor (TAP) leave the nucleus and where they position along the NPC. Increasing O-GlcNAz to enhance mRNP exporting from 35% to 61% and TAP exporting from 28% to 62%.[140]
PP7-PCP system fused to YFPmRNASingle-Particle ImagingIn Saccharomyces cerevisiae for mRNP export, which consisting of nuclear docking, transport, and cytoplasmic release from a nuclear pore complex (NPC), is fast (∼200 ms) and that upon arrival in the cytoplasm, mRNPs are frequently confined near the nuclear envelope. Mex67p functions as the principal mRNP export receptor in budding yeast.[141]
Type III-A CRISPR-Csm RNPs complexmRNASingle-Molecule Live-Cell Fluorescence ImagingBy tracking individual native NOTCH2 and MAP1B transcripts in living cells, the study identified two distinct localization mechanisms: cotranslational translocation of NOTCH2 mRNA at the endoplasmic reticulum and directional transport of MAP1B mRNA toward the cell periphery.[142]
Hrp36-labeled mRNPsmRNALight Sheet MicroscopyFor native mRNA, only ~25% of observed events resulted in successful export, and the complete export process ranged from ~65 ms to several seconds when imaged with a 20 ms exposure (50 Hz).[143]
MS2-MCP system fused to YFPmRNASuper-Registration MicroscopyThe kinetics of mRNA transport in mammalian cells present a three-step model consisting of docking (80 ms), transport (5 to 20 ms) and release (80 ms), totalling 180 ± 10 ms.[144]
MS2-MCP system fused to the Dys constructsmRNAWide-Field Epi-Fluorescence MicroscopyIn vivo, bulk mRNP transport and export, from transcription sites to the nuclear pore complex (NPC), which occurred over a 5 to 40 min timescale, with no detectable accumulation at NPCs. Once engaged with the NPC, mRNP export was rapid (~0.5 s) and kinetically faster than diffusion through the nucleoplasm.[145]
MS2-MCP system fused to YFPmRNAWide-Field Fluorescence MicroscopySingle-pore analyses of mRNA export across different transition stages showed that the initial binding of mRNPs to the NPC does not require NXF1.[146]
MS2-MCP system fused to GFPmRNASingle-Molecule RNA FISH and STED MicroscopyThe mRNP is a flexible structure during travels, with 5’-directionality during export.[147]
MS2-MCP system fused to mCherry/GFPmRNASPEED microscopy~36% of mRNPs interacting with NPC successfully complete the export process. During their ~12 ms transport time, mRNPs adopt a fast-slow-fast diffusion pattern while interacting with the periphery of the NPC and rarely enter the central axial conduit reserved for passive diffusion revealed by a 3D reconstruction of the export route for mRNPs through the NPC in live cells.[148]
Halo-tagged eIF6 to label pre-60S particlesPre-ribosomal particlesSingle Molecule Tracking (Hilo)For export events of the pre-60S ribosomal subunit, maximum particle accumulation is found in the center of the pore, while unsuccessful export terminates within the nuclear basket. The export has a single rate limiting step and a duration of ∼24 milliseconds. Only about 1/3 of attempted export events are successful. The mass flux through a single NPC can reach up to ~ 125 MDa·s − 1 in vivo.[149]
The FRET-Pairs Labeling:RpL3-GFP and RpL23-mCherry to label pre-60 s.RpS13-GFP and RpS11-mCherry to label pre-40 s.Pre-ribosomal particlesSPEED microscopy and Single-Molecule Fluorescence Resonance Energy Transfer Techniques.Pre-ribosomal particles exhibit significantly higher nuclear export efficiency compared to other large cargos like mRNAs, with around two-thirds of interactions between the pre-60S or pre-40S and the nuclear pore complexes (NPCs) resulting in successful export to the cytoplasm.CRM1plays a crucial role in nuclear export efficiency, with pre-60S and pre-40S particle export efficiency decreasing by 11 17-fold when CRM1 is inhibited.[150]
N6-adenosine methylation enhances nuclear mRNA export through METTL3 and NUP93.mRNASPEED microscopym6A methyltransferase METTL3 localizes at NPCs and functionally associates with the nucleoporin NUP93 to promote the efficient export of m6A-modified mRNPs. These findings define an m6A-METTL3-NUP93 regulatory axis for nuclear mRNA export with broad implications for human disease.[151]

sec.title: Single-molecule labelling strategies for live-cell RNA tracking
sec.p: Among all RNA types, mRNAs are unusually friendly to fluorescence in physics. As the product of genetic encoding, mRNAs can be multivalently labelled, which covers tens of fluorophores within a single transcript, boosting brightness and enabling millisecond sampling without immediately bleaching. Besides, the growing pre-mRNA combines with heterogeneous nuclear ribonucleoproteins (hnRNPs) to form large and heterogeneous assemblies as the mRNPs. In this case, exported mRNPs interact measurably with the NPC barrier, producing dwell times and step-like behaviours that are long enough to detect above background and to map spatially within the pore. In practice, ‘big and bright’ is the winning combination: it yields longer tracks, higher localization precision, and a clearer separation between successful translocation and non-productive probing.

The most widely used and best-established strategy for labelling mRNAs in single-NPC translocation studies relies on sequence-specific binding between engineered RNA stem-loops and a fluorescently tagged bacteriophage coat protein (CP) (Table 1). Representative implementations include the MS2-MCP and PP7-PCP systems, as well as related RNA-protein tagging modules such as Bgl stem-loops, λ boxB/λN, and U1A-based tagging (Table 1).

These systems were commonly deployed with GFP/YFP-tagged coat proteins (e.g. YFP-MCP or YFP-PCP) to label mRNAs [144,146,147]. Because red-emitting fluorophores are well separated from cellular autofluorescence and often offer high brightness and photostability, our lab also implemented an mCherry-MCP-based labelling scheme. In this configuration, we selectively illuminate a single GFP-labelled NPC and track individual mRNP transits through that NPC in live cells (Table 1).

Notably, insertion of MS2 stem-loops (MS2SL) or PP7 stem-loops (PP7SL) has been reported to perturb mRNA processing and subcellular localization [152]. This has motivated upgraded variants (e.g. dMCP/dPCP variants [153] or MBSV6 [154]). However, how these tagging modules quantitatively impact native export kinetics and route selection remains insufficiently characterized and warrants systematic evaluation.

Beyond stem-loop-coat-protein tagging, several approaches aim to label endogenous mRNPs with reduced perturbation. One strategy leverages hrp36 [143], an abundant RNA-binding protein that is incorporated into nascent pre-mRNPs and remains associated with mRNPs during export through the NPC [155]. In this implementation, individual endogenous mRNPs were labelled using an Alexa Fluor 647-Hrp36 fusion. To provide spatial reference for NPCs along the NE, the authors used Alexa Fluor 546-NTF2; because NTF2 is a transport receptor that binds the FG-repeats of nucleoporins with high selectivity, it localizes heavily to and effectively delineates the NPCs. Compared with MS2/PP7 coat-protein systems, Hrp36-based labelling is considered less disruptive to the native export machinery and can, in principle, accommodate endogenous transcripts of varying lengths [156] (Table 1). Another endogenous labelling route employs RNA-targeting CRISPR-Csm complexes. In this system, the Cas6 enzyme processes precursor crRNAs, which subsequently assemble with five distinct Csm proteins (Csm1-5) to form the functional ribonucleoprotein complex [157]. Native Csm is a type III-A crRNA-guided RNase that cleaves specific RNAs; however, introducing a specific mutation (D33A) into the Csm3 subunit ablates its RNase activity [158]. This catalytically inactivated Csm retains specific and durable RNA binding without cleaving the target transcript. To achieve the signal-to-noise ratio required for single-molecule resolution in living cells, frameworks such as smLiveFISH utilize this inactivated Csm complex alongside multiplexed guide RNAs, which concurrently bind a single target RNA with multiple crRNAs to amplify the fluorescent signal [142]. However, a key caveat for nucleocytoplasmic transport studies is that multi-guide recruitment adds substantial RNA-bound mass and steric bulk, which may perturb native export kinetics. Consequently, to date, these systems have seen limited use in quantifying NPC-resolved crossing kinetics at the level of docking, translocation, and release.

Additionally, fluorogenic RNA aptamers, such as Spinach, Broccoli, Corn, Mango, Coral, and Pepper, have recently emerged as an efficient alternative labelling strategy for background-free imaging of cellular RNAs. However, because fluorescence is activated primarily upon dye binding to the folded aptamer, their broader adoption has been limited by issues such as folding efficiency and fluorescence brightness or photostability in live cells. Novel Mango II has been shown marked improvements in signal to noise ratio than MS2-tdMCP-mCherry dual-labelled mRNAs, which has high contrast and single-molecule sensitivity, extended time periods of tracking single mRNAs and less effects on cellular localization [159]. Nevertheless, applications of fluorogenic aptamers for NPC-resolved export kinetics remain comparatively scarce, and their utility for quantifying pore-crossing steps (docking-translocation-release) is not yet well established.

Unlike mRNA, ribosomal RNAs are exported as the assembly intermediates of the ribosomal subunits, pre-40S and pre-60S particles, have more workable labelling strategies related to pre-ribosomal particles and associated proteins rather than direct RNA sequence modification (Table 1, Table 2). Consequently, live-cell tracking of pre-ribosomal particles predominantly relies on tagging associated transport factors or ribosomal proteins. For instance, Ruland et al. have developed a strategy by labelling a transport factor which binds pre-ribosome during nuclear stages and dissociates after export, they tried halo-tagged eIF6 labelling pre-60 s and GFP-fused NTF2 labelling NPCs in single molecule tracking ribosomal particles exploration [149] (Table 1, Table 2). Also, there is another labelling method by a ribosomal protein (RP) that is incorporated into the subunit before exporting, for example, our lab has applied a fluorescent FRET-pair labelling approach in high-speed single-molecule tracking, with two selected neighbouring r-proteins labelled with GFP and mCherry, to visualize pre-60 s and pre40 s exporting directly via expressing RPL23-mcherry and RPS11-mcherry, and POM121-GFP as the reference of NPCs [182] (Table 1).Table 2.
