Note: We here only highlight key biological insights from recent studies.

In addition, several emerging labelling strategies, such as molecular beacon-based RNA labelling [183] and spherical nucleic acid (SNA) gold nanoparticle conjugates [184], have been reported for live-cell tracking of lncRNAs or observation of snRNAs (Table 2). However, their applications to nuclear transport remain limited. For molecular beacons, key challenges [185] include background fluorescence arising from partial signals in the closed state, off-target hybridization, and the potential to perturb binding between RNA and transport factors. With continued advances in probe delivery, improved suppression of non-specific signals, and integration with super-resolution microscopy, these limitations may be mitigated, enabling broader use of more advanced labelling methods to interrogate RNA nuclear transit.

sec.title: Modalities: single-particle tracking & illumination strategies
sec.p:
RNA passage through the NPC is a rapid, stochastic process that unfolds on millisecond timescales while being confined to nanometre-scale dimensions within and around the pore. Under these conditions, static imaging is rarely sufficient to resolve transport dynamics, and the field has therefore relied heavily on high-speed single-particle tracking (SPT) [186](Figure 5a). Figure 5.
Imaging modalities used in current RNA transit studies. (A) Principle of single-molecule localization and trajectory reconstruction. An isolated fluorophore is localized by fitting its diffraction-limited point spread function (PSF), allowing its position to be determined with sub-diffraction precision. Localization precision depends on the PSF width, the number of detected photons, and background noise. Localized positions from consecutive image frames are then linked to reconstruct single-particle trajectories. (B) Epi-fluorescence microscopy. The excitation light uniformly illuminates the sample through the objective lens, providing a simple wide-field imaging mode but with higher out-of-focus background than selective illumination methods. (C) Side-view schematic of light-sheet single-molecule imaging. An elliptically shaped laser beam is introduced laterally into the sample chamber to generate a thin light sheet across the specimen. Fluorescence is collected through the detection objective, and the selective plane illumination reduces out-of-focus background and improves image contrast. (D) HILO microscopy. The excitation beam is introduced off axis through the objective to create a highly inclined illumination sheet, reducing background fluorescence while maintaining a relatively large field of view. (E) Speed microscopy. A parallel laser beam is shifted from the center of the objective and focused to form a stationary, tightly confined, inclined excitation spot at the focal plane, enabling high-speed single-molecule imaging with reduced background. (F) Principle of STED imaging. A diffraction-limited excitation beam is overlaid with a doughnut-shaped depletion beam that suppresses fluorescence at the periphery, restricting emission to a smaller central region and thereby improving spatial resolution.At the upper left of this sub-image, the text “Single molecule localization” sits above a tilted rectangular image containing a blurred spot. Directly below, a three-dimensional mesh surface is attached by a dashed vertical line to the blurred-spot image above and to a lower tilted rectangular image below that contains a single small dot, indicating a localized position. To the right, the text “Trajectories” appears above a vertical stack of several tilted rectangular frames, each frame containing multiple small dots; dashed lines connect dots between successive frames to form linked paths and the text “Consecutive frames” appears to the right of the stack beside a long arrow pointing downward, indicating frame order from top to bottom. The image B showing epi-fluorescence microscopy, with the label “B” near the upper left of this sub-image. A coordinate triad labeled “x”, “y” and “z” is placed above an objective lens drawing labeled “Objective”. A horizontal line labeled “Focal plane” runs above the objective and a dashed horizontal line lies just below it. A wide beam shape is drawn passing through the objective and narrowing toward the focal plane, indicating illumination through the objective toward the focal plane. The image C showing light-sheet single-molecule imaging, with the label “C” near the left. A coordinate triad labeled “x”, “y” and “z” is above the optics. On the left, a horizontal cylindrical element is labeled “Illumination” and emits a laterally directed beam that travels rightward as a thin sheet aligned with a horizontal line labeled “Focal plane”, with a dashed line just below that plane. Below the focal plane, a second lens drawing is labeled “Detection”, positioned under the sheet to indicate collection through the detection path. The image D showing HILO microscopy, with the label “D” near the upper left of this sub-image. A coordinate triad labeled “x”, “y” and “z” is above an objective labeled “Objective”. A horizontal line labeled “Focal plane” runs above the objective with a dashed line just below it. An off-axis beam enters through the objective and emerges as an inclined sheet that intersects the focal plane at an angle, indicating highly inclined illumination relative to the plane. The image E showing speed microscopy, with the label “E” near the left. A coordinate triad labeled “x”, “y” and “z” is above an objective labeled “Objective”. A horizontal line labeled “Focal plane” runs above the objective with a dashed line just below it. A narrow beam passes through the objective and exits as an inclined line that meets the focal plane and a curved angle marker at the intersection is labeled “45” followed by the degree symbol, indicating a forty five degree inclination. The image F showing STED imaging, with the label “F” near the upper left of this sub-image. A coordinate triad labeled “x”, “y” and “z” is above an objective labeled “Objective” and a horizontal line labeled “Focal plane” runs above it with a dashed line just below. To the left of the objective, three circular spot illustrations are stacked vertically: the top spot is labeled “STED” on one line and “Effective” then “Excitation” on subsequent lines; the middle spot is labeled “Excitation” then “Laser”; the bottom spot is labeled “STED” then “Laser”. To the lower right of the spot illustrations, a rectangular element labeled “Phase Plate” is attached to a beam path that leads into the objective and a small arrow near the focal plane points toward the region labeled “STED” and “Effective” and “Excitation”, indicating the effective excitation region at the focal plane produced by the combined excitation and STED paths.A diagram showing six microscopy schematics for single-molecule RNA transit imaging and localization.

SPT is conceptually rooted in the same single-emitter localization principle that underlies single-molecule localization microscopy (SMLM). In both cases, the point spread function (PSF) of an individual fluorophore is isolated, either spatially by sparse labelling or temporally by controlling emitter density, and the fluorescent spot is fitted (often with a 2D Gaussian) to estimate the emitter’s centroid with sub-diffraction precision [187]. The essential distinction is analytical rather than optical: whereas structural SMLM methods such as PALM and STORM aggregate many localizations into a super-resolved image [188,189], SPT preserves time ordering and reconstructs trajectories. After localizing centroids frame by frame, linking algorithms (for example, nearest-neighbour association or Kalman filtering) connect positions across time to recover the particle’s path [186] (Figure 5a). These trajectories, in turn, enable quantitative extraction of kinetic and mechanistic parameters – such as diffusion coefficients, dwell/residence times, directed biases, and state transitions – that cannot be inferred from static snapshots.

Their achievable spatiotemporal resolution is set by a photon-budget trade-off. Capturing fine-scale steps within or near the pore typically requires high frame rates on the order of >200–500 Hz [140,148,190] to minimize motion blur and to resolve brief interactions. Such rapid sampling demands high photon flux during very short exposure windows, often pushing excitation towards fluorophore saturation, which accelerates photobleaching and shortens track duration. Conversely, when the goal is to follow the broader nucleocytoplasmic journey of RNA particles over longer distances and times, experiments are often performed at lower acquisition rates (e.g. ~50–70 Hz) using reduced excitation power and a larger illumination volume (Figure 5b), sacrificing temporal resolution to extend trajectory length and improve long-term observability [144,191]. In practice, these regimes are complementary: the ‘fast’ mode is optimized for intra-pore kinetics, whereas the ‘slow’ mode is optimized for long-range context and pathway coupling.

Because localization precision in live-cell imaging is frequently limited not only by photon counts but also by background fluorescence, high-speed tracking is commonly paired with illumination strategies that suppress out-of-focus signal. Light-sheet microscopy [182] and highly inclined and laminated optical sheet (HILO) illumination [192] are particularly effective, as they confine excitation to a thin optical section (parallel or oblique relative to the imaging plane), thereby reducing background from the surrounding nucleoplasm and cytoplasm and improving signal-to-noise for single emitters (Figure 5c-d). Such background-reduction modalities have been used to track export of endogenous mRNPs labelled via specific factors (for example, an Alexa-647-hrp36 fusion protein) [143].

For maximal spatiotemporal precision at individual pores, our lab employs Single-Point Edge-Excitation Sub-Diffraction (SPEED) microscopy to study mRNP and preribosomal transport [140,148,150,190] (Figure 5e). SPEED uses a stationary, tightly focused, inclined excitation beam to selectively illuminate a single NPC at the NE in live cells, concentrating photon flux into a diffraction-limited volume matched to the pore’s spatial scale. This geometry simultaneously boosts photon yield from the tracked particle and minimizes background from the surrounding nucleoplasm, enabling exceptional temporal resolution down to ~0.4 ms per frame (up to ~2,500 Hz), while maintaining ~10–20 nm localization precision [140,148,190] (Table 1). Although SPEED measurements are intrinsically two-dimensional, the method can be paired with deconvolution strategies that exploit the approximate rotational symmetry of the NPC to reconstruct high-resolution three-dimensional transport probability maps from large ensembles of trajectories. In this way, illumination engineering and high-speed SPT become tightly integrated: optical sectioning and single-pore excitation reduce background and maximize photons per frame, while trajectory reconstruction converts these photons into kinetic and route-resolved descriptions of transport through the NPC [140,148,190]. A practical consideration in applying SPEED imaging is that very low-abundance transport targets may produce infrequent detectable translocation events. This limitation can be mitigated by using a larger illumination field, such as narrow-field epifluorescence or HILO illumination, to capture more transport events across multiple NPCs, as well as by extending acquisition times. Together, these strategies improve sampling and enable statistically robust transport maps and kinetic analyses.

Another case for confining the effective PSF well below the diffraction limit in RNA imaging is stimulated emission depletion (STED) microscopy, which utilizes a focused excitation beam and a donut-shaped depletion laser to suppress fluorescence emission at the periphery of the excitation spot [193] (Figure 5f). Conventionally, STED shared the scanning mechanism of laser scanning confocal microscopy, thus frame rate of STED microscopy depends heavily on the size of the regions of interest, with a typical small scanning area of 1–5 µm2 achieving 1–30 Hz [194]. However, the available resolution in STED is also governed by a difficult trade-off between localization and sample health. In practice, resolution improvement depends on factors such as depletion power, depletion wavelength, objective numerical aperture (NA), and the saturation characteristics of the fluorophore [195], but increasing depletion intensity also accelerates photobleaching and can induce substantial phototoxicity in live samples. To mitigate these limitations, STED is increasingly being combined with adaptive imaging methods to offer a promising path forward. For example, STED-smFISH has been successfully used to visualize nanoscale changes in mitochondrial [196] and neural mRNA [197] localization with high specificity, as well as the directional behaviour of mRNA during transit through the NPC [147].

sec.title: Quantitative metrics
sec.p: Mechanistic models of RNA export provide a natural framework for defining quantitative readouts from spatiotemporal single-molecule and super-resolution datasets. In practice, these metrics fall into three complementary classes: (i) search and engagement behaviours at the nuclear periphery, (ii) intra-pore transport kinetics inferred from single-particle motion statistics, and (iii) functional outcomes that quantify how often export succeeds and how efficiently individual pores are used.

A consistent observation from single-particle studies is that many RNAs are not exported immediately upon reaching the NE. Instead, they can undergo lateral ‘scanning’ along the nuclear periphery before committing to translocation [198]. This pre-export behaviour likely reflects regulated interactions with nuclear-basket components and RNA-binding proteins, as well as the need for an mRNP to achieve an export-competent state. Two straightforward descriptors capture this regime: the scanning time, defined as the duration of continuous RNA motion confined to the nuclear periphery prior to export, and the jump distance, defined as the inter-frame displacement during this perinuclear phase [199]. Importantly, NPCs are not uniformly spaced but occur in heterogeneous clusters; a reported peak in nearest-neighbour spacing (~240 nm) [200] is substantially larger than the ~100 nm diameter of the NPC scaffold. This geometric mismatch suggests that observed ‘jumps’ are unlikely to reflect diffusion between adjacent pore channels, and may instead report a local search process in which the cargo repositions, reorganizes, or samples distinct docking sites while probing for a pore that is accessible and/or permissive to transport.

Once an RNA engages a particular NPC, the relevant timescale is typically summarized by the dwell time, which quantifies the residence of an RNP at a single pore during a putative transport attempt. Operationally, dwell time can be estimated from the number of consecutive frames in which the particle remains within a pore-sized region centred on an NPC. Using a pore-scale threshold is practical, but the analysis should reflect the specific reference used (NPC marker, registration accuracy, and localization precision) rather than relying solely on the NPC’s nominal diameter. Conceptually, longer dwell times can arise from productive stepwise translocation, repeated binding/unbinding to FG-rich environments, or kinetic bottlenecks at docking, remodelling, or release steps, so dwell time is most informative when interpreted alongside directionality and displacement statistics.

Beyond these descriptive measures, biophysical parameters extracted from trajectories provide a more mechanistic handle on transport. The mean squared displacement (MSD) is widely used to estimate an effective diffusion coefficient and to detect deviations from Brownian motion. In NPC-associated trajectories, deviations are expected because transport is facilitated by transient interactions with receptors and nucleoporins and because the pore imposes geometric confinement. Accordingly, trajectories are often better captured by an anomalous diffusion model in which MSD scales as MSDt∝tα, where the exponent α reports the degree of subdiffusion (α<1) and the prefactor yields an effective generalized diffusivity [201]. These parameters provide a compact way to quantify how strongly motion is slowed and confined relative to free diffusion, and they enable rigorous comparisons across RNA classes, labelling strategies, perturbations, and cellular states.

NPC transport is also intrinsically anisotropic because motion is constrained by the nuclear envelope and biased along the nucleocytoplasmic axis. Directionality can therefore be quantified by comparing displacements normal to the NE (the transport axis) versus tangential displacements along the envelope. One useful measure is a diffusion anisotropy or directional ratio, defined as the effective mobility (or displacement variance) along the normal axis divided by that along the tangential axis. When combined with dwell time and anomalous diffusion parameters, anisotropy helps distinguish lateral perinuclear exploration from committed translocation and can reveal whether specific factors increase forward bias without necessarily increasing overall mobility.

Finally, export should be evaluated by outcome-based metrics that connect dynamics to function. Because RNAs can move bidirectionally within the pore environment and not all transport attempts succeed, our lab quantified export efficiency as the fraction of productive events (nuclear entry followed by cytoplasmic exit) relative to the total of productive plus abortive events, where abortive events denote RNPs that enter or dock at the NPC but return to the nucleoplasm after interaction with the nuclear basket [150]. A complementary measure is export flux, defined as the number of successful export events per NPC per unit time. Given the heterogeneous spatial organization of NPCs and the possibility of pore-to-pore variability in composition or local environment, flux is best analysed at the level of individual NPCs rather than averaged across the envelope. The resulting distribution of per-NPC flux values can test whether export is evenly shared among pores or whether a subset of ‘high-throughput’ NPCs dominates transport, providing a quantitative route to connect nanoscale dynamics with cell-scale export capacity.

sec.title: What we have learned from NPC-resolved imaging of mRNA export
sec.p: Over the past two decades, mRNA export has become the ‘default proving ground’ for single-molecule and super-resolution studies of RNA passage through NPCs. It offers an unusually favourable combination of biological importance and tractable optical readouts at the level of single export events (Table 1). The mRNP is a flexible structure during travels, with 5’-directionality during export [147], meanwhile, because mRNA export links nuclear gene expression to cytoplasmic translation – and is frequently rewired in stress responses, viral infection, and cancer – there has been strong motivation to move beyond ensemble averages towards NPC-resolved kinetics and export outcomes.

Time-resolved imaging supports a three-step framework for mammalian mRNP export – nuclear docking, translocation, and cytoplasmic release – and indicates that successful events can be completed on a sub-second timescale (~200 ms) [191], with mRNP motion through the pore faster than expected from simple diffusion in the nucleoplasm [145]. With ~20 ms time resolution, stepwise durations were estimated at ~80 ms for docking, ~5–20 ms for translocation, and ~80 ms for release, giving a total of ~180 ± 10 ms per export event [144]. Importantly, single-molecule light-sheet imaging (≈10 nm spatial precision and 20 ms frame interval) showed that productive export is not guaranteed: only ~25% of mRNP encounters with NPCs resulted in successful export, whereas ~75% were abortive, with interaction times spanning ~65 ms to several seconds [143]. Together, these studies establish a kinetic picture in which export is fast but probabilistic, and where non-productive NPC engagements are common (Table 1).

By pushing spatiotemporal resolution to ~10 nm and 0.4 ms, our lab refined the translocation step and revealed faster pore passage than previously appreciated. We measured an average NPC dwell time of ~12 ms—~10-fold shorter than earlier reports [148]—and observed a fast-slow-fast diffusion pattern across the pore. The effective diffusion coefficients were ~0.16 µm2 /s on the nucleoplasmic side, ~0.07 µm2 /s in the central scaffold, and ~0.19 µm2 /s on the cytoplasmic side [148], highlighting the central scaffold as the major region of slowed motion (Table 1).

Beyond kinetics, 3D trajectory reconstructions provide a spatial framework for where mRNPs travel within the NPC [148]. Transport is often described in terms of a central channel associated with passive diffusion and a peripheral route associated with facilitated transport, with some cargos showing mixed usage [148,202]. Consistent with this model, 3D mapping indicates that mRNPs preferentially engage the NPC periphery on the nucleoplasmic side, traverse the pore while largely avoiding the central axial conduit used for passive diffusion of small molecules, and dissociate on the cytoplasmic side [148]. Notably, even within the ~50–80 nm cytoplasmic region preceding release, mRNPs remain biased towards peripheral routes rather than switching to the axial channel [148]. Thus, export is organized not only in time (docking → translocation → release) but also in space, with reproducible non-uniform occupancy across the NPC cross-section.

Finally, NPC-resolved imaging emphasizes that export kinetics and success probability are tuned by mRNP assembly and remodelling [203]. On the nuclear side (see Section 3.8), factors that shape mRNP maturation and NPC engagement can alter outcomes: TPR inactivation has been reported to increase export of intron-retained mRNAs in mammalian cells [204], and perturbing the export receptor Mex67p (budding yeast, Saccharomyces cerevisiae) can delay productive completion of cytoplasmic release [141,205]. RNA modifications such as N6-methyladenosine (m6A) can also modulate export and broader post-transcriptional fates [206]. On the cytoplasmic face, ATP-dependent remodelling drives directionality: Dbp5 (budding yeast, Saccharomyces cerevisiae), activated by Gle1-IP6, promotes remodelling and release [207] and RanBP2 has been implicated in regulating cytoplasmic dissociation in human U2OS cells [208]. In our NPC-resolved measurements, elevating O-GlcNAc was associated with a higher export success rate (from ~35% to ~61%) [140]. More recently, through collaborations with the Lee and Xu laboratories, we further defined an m6A – METTL3–NUP93 regulatory axis for nuclear mRNA export, in which m6A markedly accelerates the export of messenger ribonucleoprotein complexes (mRNPs) from the nucleus [151]. Collectively, these findings support a unified view in which layered regulatory inputs converge on mRNP remodelling to control both the speed and the probability of successful mRNA export (Table 1).

sec.title: NPC-Resolved imaging of pre-ribosomal particle export
sec.p: Beyond single-molecule studies of mRNA export, NPC-resolved imaging has begun to quantify the export kinetics of large ribosomal subunits – pre-60S and pre-40S particles – at individual NPCs (Table 1). The eukaryotic pre-60S particle (2.1–3.1 MDa) is ~25 nm in diameter, whereas the pre-40S particle is ~16 nm on average. Given an NPC transport central channel of ~40–60 nm, simple geometry suggests that only a limited number of pre-60S particles could traverse side-by-side within the same cross-section, especially if translocation requires a preferred orientation. Nevertheless, multiple pre-60S particles can co-occupy a single NPC by queuing at different axial positions, with up to four particles estimated to fit simultaneously along the transport axis [209]. Consistent with this queuing model, single-molecule tracking revealed maximal pre-ribosomal particle accumulation near the pore centre and identified a single rate-limiting step of ~24 ± 4 ms during export [149] (Table 1).

In terms of outcomes, pre-ribosomal particle export appears comparatively efficient. Our lab observed that roughly two-thirds of pre-60S and pre-40S encounters with NPCs result in successful delivery to the cytoplasm [150]. Supporting a CRM1-dependent mechanism, pharmacological inhibition of CRM1 reduces export efficiency of both pre-60S and pre-40S particles by ~11–17-fold [150]. Structural work further explains how such large cargos achieve high-throughput export: cryo-electron microscopy of native pre-60S particles shows multiple export-associated factors bound to flexible regions and/or the subunit interface, creating multiple anchoring sites for NPC engagement [209] (Table 1). These factors are positioned to interact with FG repeats from multiple nucleoporins, which likely increases the probability of productive docking, stabilizes translocation through the FG network, and may help tune NPC plasticity and transport capacity during the passage of pre-ribosomal particles [150,209].

sec.title: Why other RNAs remain underexplored at NPC resolution
sec.p: Achieving NPC-resolved trajectories for diverse RNA classes is fundamentally limited by a recurring trade-off: the labelling density needed for sufficient photon output often perturbs the very kinetics one aims to measure. mRNA and rRNA are transported as large RNP assemblies – mRNPs and pre-60S/pre-40S particles [45,210]—that naturally recruit many RNA-binding proteins and export factors, yielding megadalton-scale complexes [211,212]. This large native mass ‘buffers’ labelling: adding multivalent tag arrays (e.g. 24× MS2/PP7 loops) or fluorescent fusion proteins (e.g. GFP-tagged ribosomal proteins) contributes relatively little additional mass compared to the cargo itself. As a result, these RNAs can tolerate high-valency labelling, enabling high photon budgets and precise localization at the kilohertz frame rates required to resolve pore transit.

Small RNAs (tRNAs, snRNAs, miRNAs, circRNAs; typically < 200 nt) lack this buffering capacity. Although bright, multivalent tagging is chemically feasible, the label can outweigh the native cargo and dominate diffusion and steric behaviour – turning the experiment into tracking the tag rather than the RNA. To preserve physiological transport, labelling is often limited to a single fluorophore or a compact aptamer, creating a severe photon bottleneck. This bottleneck is amplified by physics: small RNAs diffuse much faster than bulky mRNPs or pre-ribosomal particles, pushing experiments towards sub-millisecond exposures and thus towards higher excitation intensities – conditions that accelerate photobleaching and can eliminate signal before the RNA ever reaches the NPC.

Biology further constrains what can be observed. Unlike mRNAs and rRNAs, which must reach the cytoplasm for translation, many non-coding RNAs (e.g. snRNAs [213] and certain lncRNAs [214,215]) function primarily in the nucleus, making bona fide export events rarer than the constitutive export of mRNA. Rare events are still measurable, but they demand unusually stable imaging over long acquisition windows, with active drift correction to prevent slow mechanical drift from obscuring sparse translocations.

Even when a putative export trajectory is captured, assigning it to a specific RNA class is difficult because export machinery is shared. CRM1 exports multiple RNA classes (rRNAs, snRNAs, piRNAs, and lncRNAs), exportin-5 exports both miRNAs and tRNAs, and some lncRNAs use the same NXF1-dependent pathway as mRNA [216]. Thus, receptor identity alone rarely disambiguates cargo class. Labelling an associated RNA-binding protein also introduces ambiguity, because many RBPs are small enough to passively diffuse through the NPC (often framed as a ~ 40–60 kDa cut-off) [217]. A labelled protein traversing the pore may therefore be free protein rather than an assembled RNP, contaminating single-molecule datasets unless RNA-protein coupling is explicitly validated. This motivates dual-colour strategies that enforce physical linkage during transit, such as smFRET [218,219], where acceptor emission requires donor-acceptor proximity on the ≤10 nm scale [150].

A final barrier is conceptual. The field still lacks a standardized geometric definition of what constitutes a bona fide ‘NPC-interacting’ event. The NPC is not a hard-walled channel with a binary entrance; it is a dynamic selective barrier [23,217]. The nuclear basket scaffold extends ~75–100 nm into the nucleoplasm [220,221], while the functional FG/IDR network may extend farther, creating a diffuse interaction zone without a sharp boundary [222]. This ambiguity propagates directly into trajectory analysis. Strict criteria (e.g. counting only tracks that cross the NPC midplane) preferentially retain successful events and systematically discard early abortive interactions occurring at distal basket/IDR regions. Loose criteria (e.g. starting trajectories far from the centre to include the full IDR reach) risk including ‘bystander’ molecules that diffuse near the envelope without engaging transport machinery. Without a common spatial standard – ideally defined by functional interaction with the FG/IDR barrier rather than scaffold geometry, cross-study comparisons remain sensitive to analysis choices.

These limitations, photon economics, event rarity, cargo-identification ambiguity, and inconsistent event definitions – are not dead ends but design constraints that point to near-term opportunities. A practical next step is to focus on cytoplasmic long non-coding RNAs, such as DANCR [223] and H19 [224], which offer a favourable compromise: they are long enough (~0.8–2.3 kb) to assemble protein-rich RNPs resembling mRNPs (supporting higher-valency labelling with less kinetic distortion) and are enriched in the cytoplasm [214], increasing the frequency of observable export events.

Reporter design can also evolve beyond bulky MS2-MCP systems. CRISPR/Cas editing enables endogenous insertion of compact aptamer arrays, which can be paired with SRM-compatible fluorogenic aptamer systems such as SiRA [225] and RhoBAST [226]. Because these dyes fluoresce upon RNA binding, they reduce background while offering high photon output with a smaller steric footprint than fluorescent-protein fusions. For the smallest RNAs (e.g. miRNAs) where even aptamer arrays may be intrusive, hybrid chemo-enzymatic approaches – directly labelling defined transcripts with photostable organic dyes in vitro – can maximize photon-to-mass efficiency [227]. Delivery via minimally invasive vehicles such as lipid nanoparticles (LNPs) [228] can then decouple detectability from cargo size while limiting cellular stress.

Finally, resolving the ‘when does transport begin?’ problem likely requires multiplexed structural referencing. Rather than defining initiation by distance from an NPC centroid, future NPC-resolved studies can co-image the RNA cargo with FG/IDR-rich nucleoporins (e.g. Nup214 and Nup153) [229,230], enabling transport attempts to be defined by measurable interaction with the functional barrier itself. This shift – from geometric thresholds to interaction-defined initiation – would make kinetic benchmarking of non-canonical RNA export both more rigorous and more comparable across studies.

sec.title: Perspective and outlook
sec.p: Recent advances in super-resolution imaging have significantly strengthened our ability to visualize RNA nucleocytoplasmic transport, and the remaining ‘toolchain gap’ for NPC-resolved RNA export is narrowing. A key development is MINFLUX (Minimal Photon Fluxes), which is emerging as a powerful alternative to conventional SMLM and SPT [231]. Unlike STORM or PALM, where localization relies on estimating the centroid of a diffraction-limited emission spot, MINFLUX localizes emitters by interrogating them with a structured ‘donut’ excitation beam that contains a central intensity zero. This shifts the fundamental scaling of localization precision: in MINFLUX, precision scales with the size of the probing region, and resolution can be improved by shrinking the scan pattern length L (‘zooming’). In practice, this partially decouples achievable precision from the emission wavelength, leaving substantial headroom for future dye and probe development. Indeed, MINFLUX can achieve ~1–3 nm 3D localization with ~0.1–1 ms sampling, enabling potentially direct visualization of millisecond-scale 3D RNP transport dynamics, and multicolour implementations can support concurrent NPC-resolved imaging of import and export [232,233].

At the same time, MINFLUX imposes stricter photophysical constraints than many standard super-resolution modalities [160,191]. Because localization is inferred from detecting intensity minimum around the donut centre, fluorophores must remain exceptionally stable during the probing window. Fast flickering or nanosecond-scale intermittency can be misread as a true minimum (‘false zeros’), producing large localization failures rather than modest noise. This creates a realistic near-term outlook in which 3D MINFLUX and more established combinations of SMLM and SPT will be complementary: MINFLUX offers unmatched spatiotemporal precision when photophysics cooperates, while SMLM/SPT approaches often provide broader probe compatibility, easier multiplexing, and higher tolerance to blinking – advantages that remain valuable for diverse RNA classes and physiological perturbations.

In parallel, a super-fast volumetric imaging strategy that couples a shortened, optimized optical path with light-field detection, single-photon sensitivity, and machine-learning-based reconstruction has enabled millisecond-scale 3D imaging. It has already been used to capture nuclear dynamics in mouse embryonic stem cells and holds strong potential for future studies of RNP export [161]. Meanwhile, multiplexed live-cell RNA labelling, via aptamers, click chemistry, high-affinity probes, and improved fluorophores, continues to expand the accessible target space. Yet for most RNAs, the key triad required for NPC-resolved transport remains difficult to satisfy simultaneously: (i) specificity for the export-competent species, (ii) brightness sufficient for millisecond sampling, and (iii) minimal perturbation of native export.

A particularly promising near-term strategy is to focus on ‘intermediate-sized’ or naturally multimeric RNPs, such as selected lncRNPs, circRNPs, defined snRNP precursors, or engineered reporters, where optical observability can approach that of mRNPs and where labelling can be buffered by larger native complex size. This would allow the field to apply single-pore, step-resolved rigour to RNA classes whose export has largely been inferred from ensemble measurements. As labelling chemistries and genetically encoded tags become smaller, brighter, and more orthogonal to RNA processing, NPC-resolved imaging should expand beyond mRNA and pre-ribosomal particles to a broader range of RNA export pathways.

Furthermore, current live-cell RNA dynamic studies are generally constrained by the model organisms most amenable to these advanced imaging techniques and labelling strategies. Consequently, RNA export systems in other eukaryotic groups, such as protozoans and plants, remain largely underexplored [234,235]. Because protozoans can utilize highly divergent RNA export machineries [236,237], investigating these systems represents a critical gap in our current knowledge and an exciting future direction for RNA biology.

Despite rapid progress, core mechanistic questions remain unresolved. Do different RNA classes traverse the NPC according to shared, cross-class principles, or do they rely on cargo-specific mechanisms? How should kinetic readouts (dwell times, success probabilities, step timing) be reconciled with structural and material-state models of the NPC? In addition, reversible transport of tRNA from the cytoplasm back to the nucleus has been suggested, but it remains incompletely characterized at the single-molecule level. Moving forward, it will be important to determine how forward export and retrograde import differ in rate-limiting steps, route usage, participating factors, and overall efficiency, and whether these two directions are coupled through competition, cooperation, or feedback regulation. Given the diversity and context-dependence of RNA biology, NPC-resolved single-molecule imaging is well positioned to provide the decisive measurements needed to answer these questions.

From a biomedical standpoint, RNA nucleocytoplasmic transport occupies a pivotal control point in gene regulation: its kinetics and directionality determine when, and in what RNP state, RNAs reach the cytoplasm to support translation and signalling, linking transport dynamics to immune responses, cancer progression, and neurodegeneration. To date, most NPC-resolved imaging has focused on native conditions to establish baseline metrics such as dwell times, pathway occupancy, and export success rates. Much less is known about how these metrics are reshaped under cellular stress (e.g. infection, DNA damage, or other stress responses), during fate transitions such as differentiation and reprogramming, or in disease states, contexts in which RNA export is often remodelled. Such remodelling may involve changes in RNP composition and conformation, shifts in route usage, and alterations in the local NPC microenvironment that collectively reprogram transport dynamics. Extending NPC-resolved imaging into these physiological and pathological regimes would directly link disease-associated phenotypes to specific transport steps, helping to pinpoint actionable regulatory nodes and therapeutic targets.

ack.title: Acknowledgments
ack.p: In this review, we have made every effort to provide a comprehensive and balanced review of the relevant literature and to interpret the findings as objectively as possible. Nevertheless, given space constraints and the limits of our knowledge, we may have inadvertently overlooked some relevant studies, and we apologize for any omissions.
D.Fu, W.Yang conceptualized the manuscript. D.Fu, W.Yu, M.Tingey, F.Zhang, C.Rush, W.Yang. wrote the manuscript and contributed to figure generation.
